1:
The witnesses read this opening verse as a study in the failure of a good man's faith, and with rare unanimity they name the fault without excusing it. The despair is stated in David's own inward speech — I shall now perish one day by the hand of Saul — and the schools compete only in the severity of their diagnosis.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the tone: the prevalence of David's fear is the effect of the weakness of his faith. He pictures David reckoning up Saul's implacable malice, the treachery of the Ziphites, and the smallness of his own forces, and drawing from that melancholy sum a dark conclusion — and Henry answers him in the Lord's own words to Peter, 'O thou of little faith, wherefore dost thou doubt?' Was David not anointed to be king? Did that not carry its own assurance that he would be preserved to the kingdom? Henry marks the deeper error in the resolve that follows: consulting his own heart only, and not the ephod or the prophet, David determines to escape into Philistine country, forsaking the land of Judah where God had bidden him set up his standard (1 Samuel 22:5).

The wesleyan-arminian voices press the same charge. Wesley calls it a gross distrust of God's promise and providence, and that after repeated demonstrations of God's peculiar care; David voluntarily runs upon the very rock for which he had cursed his enemies (1 Samuel 26:19). Clarke calls the whole conclusion 'a very hasty' one: God had so often interposed for his life that David was authorized to believe the reverse.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott fills in the human background — Saul's fatal malady growing sharper, his Benjamite favourites inciting him, the memory of En-gedi and Hachilah quickly erased — and yet he too returns the same verdict: 'a dull despair seems to have at this time deprived David at once of faith and hope,' for in this whole determination 'we never hear of prayer, or of consultation with prophet or with priest.' Keil and Delitzsch, more soberly, simply record the strategy: in despair of escaping Saul's plots David betook himself to the neighbouring land, to that same Achish of Gath before whom he had once had to feign madness to save his life. Where a single note of comfort enters, it is Wesley's, and it is providential rather than exculpatory: God designed by this very flight to withdraw David from Israel, that the nation might fall by the Philistines at Gilboa without reproach to him. The witnesses land together on the point the reformed Gill states plainly — this is a fit of unbelief into which even the man after God's own heart could fall — and on Henry's closing prayer, wrung from the scene: 'Lord, increase our faith,' and 'lead us not into temptation.'
2:
The narrative's bare movement — David arose and passed over to Achish the son of Maoch, king of Gath, with his six hundred men — draws from the witnesses two quite different kinds of attention: the grammatical-historical care over persons and numbers, and the moral protest of the wesleyan-arminian school over the deed itself.

On the facts, the grammatical-historical commentators are agreed and precise. Keil and Delitzsch identify Achish the son of Maoch with the king of the same name before whom David had fled earlier (1 Samuel 21:11), and with the Achish son of Maachah of a later reign (1 Kings 2:39), granting that this would require a reign of some fifty years — long, but not impossible. Ellicott concurs on the identification and adds a note on the six hundred: this was the original number and still the nucleus of the force, but the total was now far larger, many a group of warriors having joined the renowned standard of the future king; he observes too that the whole of Philistia would afterward fall under David's rule, though he seems to have let Achish keep his old city of Gath as a tributary — repaying, as it were, the old debt of kindness.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke will have none of this smoothed over. 'There is not one circumstance in this transaction that is not blameable,' he writes: David joins the enemies of his God and of his country, will shortly act an inhuman part against the Geshurites and Amalekites without even the pretence of divine authority, and will lie to his protector. Clarke is careful to distinguish record from approval — 'the inspired penman tells what he did, but passes no eulogium upon his conduct; and it is false to say that, because these things are recorded, therefore they are approved' — and he presses the hard verdict that in all these transactions David 'was in no sense a man after God's own heart.' He dismisses Chandler's attempted defence with a shrug: those who can receive it, let them.

The reformed Gill, for his part, does not linger, treating the verse chiefly as the hinge that carries David out of the land. Between the two emphases there is no real contradiction but a difference of office: the grammatical-historical school anchors the episode in verifiable history — the same king, the same city, the swelling band — while the wesleyan-arminian conscience refuses to let the reader mistake a recorded flight for a sanctioned one. Both, in the end, serve the same honesty about Scripture that Clarke insists on: it narrates the sins of its saints without gilding them, so that David's crossing of the frontier stands as warning, not pattern.
3:
David dwelt with Achish at Gath, he and his men, every man with his household — David himself with his two wives, Ahinoam the Jezreelitess and Abigail the widow of Nabal. The witnesses read the settling-in for its domestic realism and, above all, for the strange reversal it represents in David's relation to Gath.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws the contrast that governs the verse. David's reception among the Philistines was most kindly, and there was a wide difference between this visit and his former one: then he came a fugitive, almost unattended, and had to escape by feigning madness; now he comes at the head of an army of trained and devoted soldiers. Such a guest might be of the greatest service to the Philistines in their perpetual wars with Saul, with whom David would now be reckoned to have finally broken off all relations, having sought shelter among the most bitter of his foes. Keil and Delitzsch add the plain administrative fact — Achish allotted dwelling-places in his capital for David, his wives, and all his retinue — and note the immediate consequence in the next verse, that Saul, hearing of the flight to Gath, desisted from any further pursuit.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke fastens on the little phrase 'every man with his household.' It appears, he observes, that the men who consorted with David had wives and families; David and his company 'resembled a tribe of the wandering Arabs' — a whole migratory community, not a mere war-band, now transplanted onto pagan soil. The reformed Gill supplies the topographical gloss that sharpens the irony: Gath was the metropolis, the royal city of the Philistine kingdom, so that the anointed of Israel now keeps house in the very capital of Israel's enemy.

The two wives quietly recall the recent past — Abigail taken after the death of Nabal (1 Samuel 25:42) and Ahinoam of Jezreel — reminding the reader that David carries into exile not only soldiers but a family, and the responsibility for their safety and their faith. The witnesses do not divide here; together they hold a sober double truth. On the surface all is well: a warm welcome, assigned houses, respite from Saul. Beneath it lies the danger the following verses will unfold — that the peace is bought at the price of alliance with the enemy, and that a righteous soul dwelling in the royal city of the Philistines is a soul continually vexed by the idolatry around it. The kindness of Achish is real; so is the compromise it seals.
4:
The single sentence — it was told Saul that David was fled to Gath, and he sought no more again for him — the witnesses read as the immediate, providential fruit of David's flight, and around it the grammatical-historical school gathers several smaller observations.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the force of the notice: this short statement tells us plainly that up to the very moment Saul heard David had crossed the frontier, he had not ceased to pursue him and seek his life. The flight, whatever its sin, at once achieved its immediate object — Saul let the matter drop. Ellicott then adds a musical aside from Ewald: it may have been during this residence among the Philistines that David exercised himself in the Gittite, that is Philistine, style of music, which he afterward carried over into Judah and Jerusalem; the feminine adjective 'Gittith' derived from Gath survives in the titles of several psalms 'upon the Gittith' (Psalms 8; Psalms 81; Psalms 84), whether the word names a Philistine measure, a manner of singing, or an instrument.

Jamieson, Fausset and Brown turn instead to the manners of the East that lie behind David's welcome. According to the usages of nomadic peoples, the duty of receiving the stranger in the sheik's absence devolves on his wife; and the moment the stranger is admitted into the tent, his claim to be defended or concealed from his pursuers is established. The observation illuminates the security David now enjoys: hospitality, once granted, was a bond the Philistine would not lightly break, which is precisely why Saul's pursuit could safely be abandoned — David was now under a protection Saul could not reach.

The reformed Gill contributes the small realistic touch that Saul very probably kept spies out to watch David's motions and report where he was and what he was doing; it was by such means, not by rumour only, that the king learned of the flight and gave over the chase. Keil and Delitzsch note the fact without elaboration. The voices do not contend over this verse; each adds a different lamp to the same scene. Together they show a strange mercy folded into a compromised choice: David gains rest from his relentless enemy, and the reader is reminded — through the psalm-titles Ellicott recalls and the hospitality laws the harmonists describe — that even these darkest months among the Philistines were not wholly barren, for out of them came security, and perhaps some of the very songs by which the church still worships. Yet the rest is Saul's ceasing to seek, not David's ceasing to fear.
5:
David's request — let them give me a place in some town in the country, that I may dwell there: for why should thy servant dwell in the royal city with thee? — the witnesses read as a masterstroke of prudence, and they divide gently only over how much of that prudence was piety and how much was policy.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry admires the request as at once wise and modest. It was really prudent: David knew what it was to be envied in the court of Saul, and had much more reason to fear it in the court of Achish, and so he declines preferment there and wishes for a quieter settlement in the country, where he might be more within himself and less in other people's way, with freer exercise of his religion for himself and his men. And it was modestly framed — he prescribes no place, only begs some town, for 'beggars must not be choosers.' Henry draws the lesson broadly: 'those that would stand fast must not covet to stand high,' and 'there is nothing lost by humility and modesty, and a willingness to retire,' for real advantages follow those who flee from imaginary honours.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the same double motive, weighted toward the pastoral. The request was 'a prudent desire,' by which David designed to preserve his people both from the vices to which conversation with the Philistines would expose them and from the envy and malice that difference of religion might provoke; and the disclaimer 'with thee' is humble courtesy — such nearness were too great an honour for a servant, too burdensome to the king, and a possible offence to his people.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott, less concerned with David's soul than with his strategy, exposes the policy beneath the courtesy. The real reason David wished a separate residence, he argues, was that he might conduct his forays and other affairs apart from the supervision of his Philistine friends: they had one purpose in welcoming him and his band, he had quite another. While the Philistine nation was preparing the great national effort against Saul that would culminate at Gilboa, David meant, from a comparatively secure centre of operations, to harry the nomad foes of Israel to the south. Clarke agrees on the practical result — two princely establishments in one city were too many, Achish felt the propriety of the proposal, and Ziklag was the answer. The debate is friendly and fine: whether the moving spring of the petition was chiefly devout self-preservation (Henry and Wesley) or chiefly a cover for independent action (Ellicott). All grant that both were present, and that the humility which asks for the lower place is repaid, in the next verse, with a town of David's own.
6:
Then Achish gave him Ziklag that day, wherefore Ziklag pertaineth unto the kings of Judah unto this day. The witnesses attend to two things: the providence by which a lost inheritance returns to Israel, and the small closing clause that has become a quiet witness to the book's own date.

On the providence, the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry is warmest. By this grant, he says, Israel recovered an ancient right, for Ziklag lay within the lot of Judah (Joshua 15:31) and was afterward assigned to Simeon (Joshua 19:5); whether never fully subdued or seized by the Philistines in some struggle, it now returns to its rightful owners — 'the righteous God judgeth righteously.' David meanwhile gains a commodious settlement, at a distance from Gath yet bordering upon Israel, where he could keep up correspondence with his own countrymen against the revolution now approaching. Henry's refrain is characteristic: 'there is nothing lost by humility and modesty,' for the man who asked for a country town is given a city that becomes a possession of the crown.

The grammatical-historical and reformed voices agree on the history and press the famous clause. Keil and Delitzsch note that Ziklag had been given to the Simeonites in Joshua's day (Josh. 19:5), later fell to the Philistines, and was probably left uninhabited by that conquest, so that Achish had it to give. Gill adds the tradition, from Bunting, that it lay twelve miles from Gath. On 'unto this day,' Clarke and Ellicott read the phrase as a note of the book's composition: Clarke takes it as proof that the book was written long after Samuel, formed by a later hand out of contemporary materials; Ellicott dates the clause more exactly, after the revolt of Jeroboam and before the carrying away of Israel to Babylon, since only then did 'the kings of Judah' name a line distinct from the kings of Israel.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley combines both concerns. Achish gave Ziklag not merely to inhabit but to possess, the more to oblige so useful a servant; being given by a Philistine to David, it thereafter belonged not to the tribe of Judah but to the king of Judah, David and his heirs for ever; and the clause 'to this day,' he agrees, was added after the main substance of the book was written. The witnesses do not divide. Together they find in this one town a double lesson: that God quietly restores to his people what their enemies had usurped, repaying David's modest petition with a lasting inheritance; and that the sacred historian's own passing phrase honestly discloses the later hand through which the record has reached the church.
7:
The chronicler's exact note — the time David dwelt in the country of the Philistines was a full year and four months — the witnesses treat as a small hinge on which large questions turn: the trustworthiness of the narrative, and the darkness of the season it measures.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott, following Keil and Delitzsch, makes the precision itself an argument. Keil calls attention to the exact statement of time as a proof of the historical character of the whole narrative; and Ellicott parses the peculiar Hebrew, in which the word rendered 'a year' is literally 'days' (yamim), a collective term used elsewhere for a period of days amounting to a full year (Leviticus 25:29; 1 Samuel 1:3). A fabricator does not trouble to date his fictions to the month; the very oddity of the reckoning speaks for its authenticity.

But Ellicott will not let the exactness pass without moral reckoning, and here his comment rises to lament. This year and four months, he writes, were among the darkest days of David's life. He was sorely tried, it is true; but he had adopted the very course his bitterest foes would have wished him to choose. In open arms, apparently leagued with the deadliest enemies of Israel, 'like an Italian condottiere or captain of free lances of the Middle Ages,' he had taken service and accepted the wages of that very Philistine city whose champion he had once slain in the morning of his career. 'At last his enemies at the court of Saul had reason when they spoke of him as a traitor.' Ellicott's honesty keeps both sides: even in this compromised service David used his power to help and not to injure his countrymen — but the price he paid for that hidden patriotism was 'a life of falsehood, stained, too, with deeds of fierce cruelty, shocking even in these rough, half-barbarous times.'

The reformed Gill and Keil and Delitzsch add little beyond confirming the sum, content to let the number stand. Yet in their reticence and Ellicott's eloquence alike the witnesses land on one point. The verse is not idle bookkeeping. It fixes the length of a fall — sixteen months in which the anointed king lived by the sword among idolaters, sheltered by a lie — and it does so in the same sober, verifiable style with which Scripture records the triumphs, so that the reader may neither doubt the history nor soften the sin. The exactness that proves the story true is the exactness that will not let David's worst season be forgotten.
8:
David and his men went up and raided the Geshurites, the Gezrites, and the Amalekites — nations that of old inhabited the land toward Shur and Egypt. The witnesses divide sharply here, and the division runs along a familiar seam: whether David's war was righteous execution of an old divine sentence, or a lawless raid that only his lie to Achish made possible.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry mounts the fullest defence. He would acquit David of injustice and cruelty in the action, 'because those people whom he cut off were such as heaven had long since doomed to destruction, and he that did it was one whom heaven had ordained to dominion.' It was not for the Lord's anointed to sit still in sloth; David avenged an old quarrel God had with these nations and fetched provisions for his army at once. The Amalekites especially were to be wholly cut off — Saul had been rejected for sparing them, and 'David makes up the deficiency of his obedience before he succeeds him.' Yet Henry is scrupulous to withhold from David's dealing with Achish the approval he grants the war: the dissimulation that follows he cannot acquit, and he hears in it the groan of the psalm, 'Remove from me the way of lying.'

The grammatical-historical commentators concentrate on geography and occasion. Keil and Delitzsch note that the occasion of the attack is unmentioned but is surely to be sought in plundering incursions these tribes made into Israel — David would hardly have risked so provocative a war without such cause; and they gloss 'went up' as the standard idiom for an army's advance (Joshua 8:1). Ellicott confirms that 'went up' is strictly accurate, the nomad tribes dwelling on the higher plateau north-east of the desert of Paran; he identifies them as Bedaween, the standing scourge of Israel's southern families, the Amalekites being the remnant of the tribe Saul had failed to destroy, and David's raids reaching to the very frontier of Egypt.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley agrees on the last point — these Amalekites were the survivors of Saul's earlier war (1 Samuel 15:3) who had retired into remote desert places. The debate, then, is not over the facts but over the verdict: to Henry the raid is the anointed king doing at last what the rejected king would not, a work of providence and provision; to Ellicott it is a ferocious foray whose barbarity the next verses will lay bare. Both agree that whatever be said of the war, the deception woven around it belongs to the darkest thread of David's story.
9:
David smote the land, left neither man nor woman alive, took the flocks and herds, the asses, camels, and apparel, and returned to Achish. Over this verse of total destruction the witnesses are most openly at odds, and the fault-line is again between the puritan reading of righteous judgment and the grammatical-historical protest against barbarity without excuse.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke states the grim fact and its motive plainly: here was a complete extirpation, not one left alive 'lest he should carry tidings of the disasters of his country' to Achish; the spoil consisted of sheep, oxen, asses, camels, and apparel. Wesley narrows the slaughter geographically — David left none alive only in the part where he came, for more Amalekites survived elsewhere, as the later sack of Ziklag would prove.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott refuses every softening. These acts of ferocious barbarity, he writes, are simply without excuse; the reason for the killing is given in the eleventh verse — no captive must be left to tell the tale to a deceived Achish, and by this cruelty David was burning his last bridge of retreat into Israel. Ellicott grants that there were occasions when a war of extermination was divinely commended, 'then Israel was simply the stern instrument of wrath'; but 'David had no such charge.' He even wonders whether it was not these ruthless deeds that left on David's hands the stain of blood which in after days rendered him unfit to build the house of the Lord he so longed to erect (1 Chronicles 28:3).

Yet Ellicott balances the darkness with a countervailing light from the chronicler. Even now brave and discontented spirits from every part of Israel were joining David at Ziklag; the roll of them fills a chapter, and among them the Benjamite Amasai cried, 'We are on thy side, thou son of Jesse... for thy God helpeth thee' (1 Chronicles 12:18), until the band grew 'a great host, like the host of God' (1 Chronicles 12:22). The people were coming to feel, in the very season of David's deepest compromise, that the Lord had indeed chosen him. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill record the razzia without comment, letting the verbs speak. The witnesses thus leave the reader holding two truths in tension that they will not dissolve: the deed was a merciless slaughter designed to protect a lie, and yet the God who judged the sin was even then gathering to this flawed man the host that would make him king. Grace, they imply, ran alongside guilt, but never once excused it.
10:
Achish asked, Whither have ye made a road today? and David answered, Against the south of Judah, and against the south of the Jerahmeelites, and against the south of the Kenites. The witnesses read the exchange as the moment David's course of blood becomes also a course of settled falsehood, and the wesleyan-arminian conscience feels it most keenly.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley calls the words 'ambiguous, and contrary to that simplicity which became David, both as a prince, and as an eminent professor of the true religion.' What aggravates the sin, he adds, is the very fidelity of Achish and the confidence the king reposed in him; and he reads David's later prayer as penitent reflection upon just this scene — 'Remove from me the way of lying.' Clarke keeps closer to the surface, inferring from the king's question that David had by now made such predatory excursions a habit, so that Achish asks as of a familiar practice.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott lays bare the mechanics of the deceit. David's answer to his sovereign lord — for he was now, to all intents, a vassal prince of Achish — 'was simply a falsehood.' He had in fact been raiding the old Bedaween enemies of Israel far away toward Egypt, and from those rich nomads had gathered much booty, a share of which he brought to his suzerain; but he represents the cattle and apparel as taken from his own countrymen. Ellicott identifies the peoples David names to give the lie its colour: the Jerahmeelites were descendants of Jerahmeel, the firstborn of Hezron, and so one of the great Judahite families (1 Chronicles 2:9; 1 Chronicles 2:25), while the Kenites were a race living in friendship with and under the protection of Judah.

The genius of the deception lay in its ambiguity — David had indeed struck peoples who lay to the south, but Achish was meant to hear 'the south of Judah,' his own countrymen, when David meant the tribes to the south of Judah. The reformed Gill draws out that same double meaning: David imposed upon Achish by ambiguous terms, insinuating that he had fallen upon the southern parts of Judah when he had really been against foreign nations lying to the south of it. The witnesses do not divide over the fact; with one voice they call it a lie, however cleverly framed, and they refuse the comfort of the equivocation. Wesley's verdict stands for them all: that a servant of the true God should traffic in such calculated ambiguity is matter for repentance, not admiration — and David himself, they believe, came at last to see it so.
11:
David saved neither man nor woman alive to bring tidings to Gath, lest they should tell on us, saying, So did David. The witnesses read the verse as the plain confession of a motive — the slaughter served the lie — and they set it in the larger frame of David's decline.

The reformed Gill wrestles with the harshness. He is reluctant to think all were simply massacred, and canvasses the view of Abarbanel that these Geshurites and their neighbours were themselves haters and enemies of Achish, so that smiting them was no treachery against David's host; otherwise, Gill says, the deed would have been such perfidy and ingratitude 'as must have made the name of David to stink.' His preferred sense is narrower — that David did not carry the captives to Gath, where they would have told whence they came and contradicted his story. Keil and Delitzsch read the killing more directly as the wall behind which the deception stood: Achish believed David, reckoning that he had made himself utterly abhorred among his own people and so would be a servant for ever.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott offers the fullest moral and historical setting, and it is unsparing. The verse and the next, he says, give the reason for 'these atrocious acts of murder,' in which 'the wild and irresponsible Arab chief alone seemed represented in David.' He notices the timing with care: this saddest of all the chapters follows close upon the death of Samuel, whose lifelong influence over the son of Jesse had been great and beneficent; when the man of God passed away, 'other and less wise counsellors prevailed with David.' Want of trust in God and a craven fear for his own life — Ellicott points back to David's own words on the hill of Hachilah (1 Samuel 26:20; 1 Samuel 26:24) — drove him out of the land, and there 'one sin led on to another,' the duplicity of his position forcing the cruelty that alone could hide it.

Ellicott adds a textual observation the others share: the closing clause 'so did David' is better read not as the imagined testimony of slaughtered captives but as the narrator's own remark on David's manner throughout his stay, since the Hebrew verb is a past and the ancient versions punctuate it so. The witnesses do not divide over the substance. Whether one softens the scene with Gill or presses it with Ellicott, all agree that a good man, having left off trusting God, had sunk to killing that he might not be found out — and that the loss of a wise counsellor and the grip of fear are the road by which even the anointed can travel so far down.
12:
Achish believed David, saying, He hath made his people Israel utterly to abhor him; therefore he shall be my servant for ever. With only two of the witnesses speaking at length, the chapter closes on the success of the deception — and on their refusal to let that success be admired.

The reformed Gill states simply how the lie landed. Achish believed David as David wished him to understand the story, imposed upon 'by ambiguous terms,' insinuating that he had been against the southern parts of Judah when he had really been against foreign nations lying to the south of Judah. The king's satisfaction is complete and, to him, reasonable: a man who has made himself odious to his own nation has nowhere left to turn, and must be a faithful vassal for ever. Keil and Delitzsch, commenting on the same words, catch the grim irony of that 'for ever' — Achish counts on a permanence that the very next chapters will overturn, for the servant he thinks bound to him for life will soon be delivered from the entanglement, and will reign over the people he is supposed to have made his enemies.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke turns the verse into a warning against every apology for such conduct. He notes that Dr. Delaney had called this deception one that 'did harm to nobody,' and confesses himself unable to share the verdict: the falsehood 'had the most direct tendency to make Achish imagine himself secure while in the utmost danger, and to have a faithful friend and able ally in David, while he was the veriest enemy he could possibly have.' Clarke's indignation is open — 'shame on him who becomes the apologist of such conduct!' — and he lays down the principle that governs his whole reading of the chapter: 'no lie is of the truth, and all falsity is an abomination to the Lord.'

The two voices, from schools often at odds, meet here without dispute. Gill exposes the mechanics of the deceit and Clarke its morality, and neither will let the reader mistake Achish's confidence for David's vindication. The chapter ends, as it began, on the theme of a faith that has faltered: David, who opened it saying in his heart that he would perish by the hand of Saul, closes it secure in the trust of a Philistine won by a lie. The witnesses leave the irony standing as their last word — that the safety purchased by falsehood is no safety, and that the God who is truth was even then preparing, in his own way, to bring his servant home.
